should

(

)
 
Introduction:
 
Cancer-associated
 
fibroblasts
 
(CAFs)
 
are
 
key
 
regulators
 
of
 
the
 
tumor
 
microenvironment
 
(TME),
 
but
 
how
 
they
 
drive
 
immune
 
evasion
 
and
 
shape
 
therapy
 
responses
 
remains
 
unclear.
 
We
 
combined
 
spatial
 
and
 
single-cell
 
transcriptomics
 
with
 
functional
 
ex-vivo
 
assays
 
to
 
define
 
therapeutically
 
relevant
 
CAF
 
phenotypes
 
and
 
assess
 
their
 
impact
 
on
 
anti-TROP2
 
antibody-drug
 
conjugates
 
(ADCs)
 
and
 
immunotherapy
 
responses.
 
 
Methodology:
 
We
 
employed
 
GeoMX
 
spatial
 
transcriptomics
 
(N
 
=
 
40,
 
AOIs
 
=
 
705)
 
of
 
FFPE
 
HGSC
 
tumors
 
to
 
define
 
spatially
 
distinct
 
fibroblast
 
programs.
 
We
 
generated
 
a
 
tumor–stroma
 
interface
 
(TSI)–associated
 
signature
 
and
 
projected
 
it
 
onto
 
scRNA-seq

define
 
spatially
 
distinct
 
fibroblast
 
programs.
 
We
 
generated
 
a
 
tumor–stroma
 
interface
 
(TSI)–associated
 
signature
 
and
 
projected
 
it
 
onto
 
scRNA-seq
 
data
 
from
 
HGSC
 
tumors
 
(N
 
=
 
29)
 
to
 
refine
 
CAF
 
states.
 
We
 
then
 
scored
 
each
 
fibroblast
 
based
 
on
 
signature
 
expression
 
and
 
annotated
 
them
 
as
 
TSI-like
 
or
 
stroma-like
 
and
 
performed
 
pathway
 
analyses
 
in
 
both
 
datasets.
 
We
 
tested
 
ADCs
 
and
 
immunotherapies
 
in
 
patient-derived
 
3D
 
HGSC
 
TME
 
models
 
+/-
 
CAFs
 
to
 
evaluate
 
how
 
CAF
 
states
 
modulate
 
responses
 
using
 
scRNA-seq
 
and
 
flow-cytometry.
 
 
Results:
 
Spatial
 
transcriptomics
 
revealed
 
a
 
distinct
 
TGF
β-
driven
 
CAF
 
program
 
enriched
 
for
 
extracellular
 
matrix
 
organization,
 
cell
 
adhesion,
 
and
 
tissue
 
remodeling
 
pathways
 
at
 
the
 
TSI.
 
Projection
 
of
 
the
 
spatial
 
gene
 
signature
 
onto
 
scRNA-seq
 
data
 
revealed
 
that
 
the
 
TSI-CAFs
 
expressed
 
predominantly
 
myCAF
 
phenotypes.
 
Spatial
 
immune
 
deconvolution
 
showed
 
that
 
TSI-CAFs
 
associated
 
with
 
significantly
 
decreased
 
infiltration
 
of
 
NK
 
cells,
 
B
 
cells,
 
CD8+
 
and
 
CD4+
 
T
 
cells
 
suggesting
 
an
 
immune-excluded
 
interface
 
driven
 
by
 
CAFs.
 
This
 
spatial

immune-exclusion
 
was
 
also
 
associated
 
with
 
higher
 
expression
 
of
 
TROP2
 
in
 
cancer
 
cells,
 
providing
 
a
 
rationale
 
for
 
TROP2-targeted
 
ADCimmunotherapy
 
combination
 
strategies.
 
To
 
functionally
 
assess
 
how
 
TSI-CAFs
 
modulate
 
therapeutic
 
responses
 
to
 
these
 
agents
 
ex-vivo,
 
we
 
established
 
and
 
validated
 
a
 
modular
 
3D
 
HGSC
 
TME
 
model
 
enabling
 
controlled
 
inclusion
 
or
 
exclusion
 
of
 
CAFs,
 
allowing
 
evaluation
 
of
 
responses
 
to
 
anti-TROP2
 
ADCs
 
and
 
immunotherapies.
 
 
Conclusion:
 
myCAFs
 
create
 
an
 
immune-suppressed
 
TSI
 
associated
 
with
 
higher
 
ADC
 
target
 
expression.
 
This
 
highlights
 
the
 
importance
 
of
 
combining
 
ADCs
 
with
 
immunotherapies
 
to
 
re-activate
 
HGSC
 
anti-tumor
 
immunity
